examples.title: Float Declaration and Initialization
examples.description: Demonstrates correct and incorrect ways to declare float variables
examples.explanation: Float literals must have 'f' or 'F' suffix because decimal literals default to double in Java. Without the suffix, the compiler treats the literal as double, causing a type mismatch when assigning to float.

examples.title: Floating-Point Precision Issue Demonstration
examples.description: Demonstrates the precision problem with floating-point arithmetic
examples.explanation: Due to binary representation limitations, floating-point arithmetic is not exact. 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented precisely in binary, so their sum is slightly more than 0.3. Always use tolerance-based comparison for floating-point equality checks.

examples.title: Special Values: NaN and Infinity
examples.description: Demonstrates handling of NaN and Infinity in Java
examples.explanation: Java follows IEEE 754 standard for special floating-point values. Infinity results from dividing by zero, NaN from undefined operations. Important: NaN is never equal to itself, so always use isNaN() method for checking.

examples.title: Scientific Notation in Java
examples.description: Demonstrates scientific notation for floating-point literals
examples.explanation: Scientific notation uses 'e' or 'E' followed by an exponent (positive or negative). The exponent is base 10. For float literals with scientific notation, the 'f' or 'F' suffix is still required.
